description: This figure presents SEM fractographs comparing the fracture surfaces of uncharged and hydrogen-charged Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C austenitic low-density steel tensile samples tested at 295 K. Subfigures (a) and (d) show low-magnification overviews of the uncharged and hydrogen-charged (101 mass ppm H) specimens, respectively, with rectangular frames indicating regions magnified in (b, c) and (e, f). The uncharged sample exhibits fully ductile fracture characterized by dimples ranging from 2-8 μm in size. The hydrogen-charged sample maintains a ductile fracture mode but displays refined dimple sizes of 1.5-3.0 μm, attributed to void sheets generated by hydrogen-enhanced strain localization phenomena. The absence of intergranular or quasi-cleavage features in the charged sample indicates that hydrogen does not promote grain boundary cracking, supporting the high hydrogen embrittlement resistance of this steel. The microvoid coalescence mechanism persists in both conditions, with hydrogen refining the fracture surface topography through enhanced dislocation activity and localized plasticity.
